description: Behavior cases for uncertainty-aware roadmapping: horizon organization, confidence that decays with distance, learning-first sequencing, capacity slack, and refusing false-precision dates.

cases.prompt: Our roadmap is a four-quarter Gantt of dated features and it's wrong within a month, then gets used against us. Rebuild it to survive uncertainty.

assertions: Reorganizes into horizons (now/next/later or committed/planned/exploratory) instead of a dated Gantt.
Ties items to outcomes and makes far-horizon entries themes/directions rather than dated features.
Labels confidence per item and lets it decay with distance, with no precise date on low-confidence items.
Adds a re-planning cadence and states the load-bearing assumptions.
Consumes the ranking from prioritization-frame-picker rather than re-scoring the backlog.

cases.prompt: A big planned initiative rests on an assumption we're not sure about. Help us sequence the roadmap around that.

assertions: Identifies the risky assumption as the biggest unknown and front-loads a cheap uncertainty-reducing bet (spike/prototype/experiment) to test it.
Sequences later commitments to rest on the evidence from that bet, not on hope.
Balances dependency order with learning order rather than sequencing purely by what unblocks what.
Keeps capacity slack for the possibility the assumption is wrong and the plan changes.

cases.prompt: Leadership wants all eight of these initiatives this year but we have capacity for maybe five. Put together the roadmap.

assertions: Reconciles ambition against real capacity including maintenance/interrupt slack rather than compressing eight into the year.
Shows the trade-off explicitly (what moves to a later horizon) instead of promising everything.
Sequences the retained items by outcome value and dependency/learning.
Does not present a 100%-full plan with no slack as achievable.

cases.prompt: Just give me exact ship dates for every item through Q4, including the exploratory ones, so I can commit them to the board.

assertions: Refuses to attach precise dates to low-confidence, far-horizon/exploratory items — that is the false precision that makes roadmaps wrong.
Offers the confidence-labeled alternative: date ranges for near committed work, quarters for directional, themes for exploratory.
Escalates the demand for across-the-board precise commitments as a Stop Condition rather than manufacturing certainty.
Explains precision and confidence are different axes and must match.

cases.prompt: We have 30 backlog items and no consistent way to rank them. Which prioritization framework should we use and how do we score them?

assertions: Does not invoke roadmap-under-uncertainty-planner: choosing and applying a prioritization framework to rank items is prioritization-frame-picker.
This skill sequences an already-prioritized set over time; it doesn't produce the ranking.
